pairs.question: What profession did Arthur Eades initially train for as a young man?
pairs.answer: Arthur Eades completed an apprenticeship as a cabinet maker after starting work as a half-timer at the age of eight.

pairs.question: In which year did Arthur Eades become president of the Birmingham Trades Council?
pairs.answer: Arthur Eades became president of the Birmingham Trades Council in 1895, having served as vice-president since 1893.

pairs.question: What significant organizational role did Arthur Eades play in advocating for old age pensions in 1896?
pairs.answer: In 1896, Eades led the organizing committee that established the National Committee of Organised Labour for Promoting Old Age Pensions for All.

pairs.question: How did Arthur Eades contribute to education in Birmingham despite not being a politician?
pairs.answer: Eades raised funds for scholarships to the University of Birmingham and became the only working-class member of its Board of Governors.

pairs.question: What political party did Arthur Eades primarily align with during his career, and why did he later distance himself?
pairs.answer: Eades was willing to work with the Labour Representation Committee (founded in 1900) but remained unenthusiastic about it. From 1902, he focused on the Birmingham Co-operative Society and ran a final unsuccessful campaign in 1904 as a Liberal Party candidate.

pairs.question: Can you list the key leadership positions held by Arthur Eades in trade unions and labor organizations?
pairs.answer: Key positions held by Arthur Eades include: president of his branch of the Alliance Cabinet Makers' Society, vice-president (1893) and president (1895) of the Birmingham Trades Council, secretary of his union branch (1896), and secretary of the trades council (1898).

pairs.question: What personal achievement unrelated to his trade union work is mentioned about Arthur Eades?
pairs.answer: Arthur Eades was known as a marksman and won numerous prizes for his rifle shooting.
